title: Revenue Cycle Controls and Risks
description: Master controls over the order-to-cash process

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The revenue cycle is a high-risk area frequently tested on the CIA exam. It spans from customer order through cash collection and involves significant fraud and error risks. Domain III (40% of Part 2) emphasizes business processes.

sections.title: Revenue Cycle Stages
sections.content:
**Order-to-Cash Process:**

1. **Sales Order Processing**
   • Customer order receipt and validation
   • Credit check and approval
   • Inventory availability confirmation

2. **Fulfillment/Shipping**
   • Order picking and packing
   • Shipping documentation
   • Transfer of title

3. **Billing**
   • Invoice generation
   • Revenue recognition
   • Customer statement preparation

4. **Collections**
   • Accounts receivable management
   • Cash receipts processing
   • Aging analysis and follow-up

sections.title: Key Controls by Stage
rows: Credit limits, credit checks before shipping

sections.title: Critical Segregation of Duties
sections.content:
**Must Be Separated:**

• **Credit approval** vs. **Sales order entry**
  (Prevents extending credit to related parties)

• **Shipping** vs. **Billing** vs. **Accounts receivable**
  (Prevents unrecorded shipments, fictitious sales)

• **Cash receipts** vs. **Accounts receivable**
  (Prevents lapping, misappropriation)

• **Customer master file maintenance** vs. **Credit limits**
  (Prevents unauthorized credit extensions)

sections.title: Revenue Recognition Fraud Indicators
sections.content:
**Watch for these red flags:**

• Channel stuffing (excessive sales to distributors)
• Bill and hold transactions
• Side agreements modifying terms
• Unusual sales near period end
• Revenue without proper performance obligation fulfillment
• Multiple element arrangements not properly allocated

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Revenue cycle spans order through cash collection
Credit approval must be segregated from sales processing
Cash receipts must be segregated from AR recordkeeping
Period-end revenue transactions require extra scrutiny
Controls should address both error and fraud risks

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following segregation of duties is MOST effective in preventing the concealment of fictitious sales?
options: Credit approval vs. Sales order entry
Shipping vs. Billing vs. Accounts receivable
Cash receipts vs. Accounts payable
Inventory management vs. Shipping
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Separating shipping, billing, and accounts receivable duties prevents fictitious sales. If one person controls all three functions, they could ship goods without proper authorization, create a billing record, and then manage the accounts receivable to conceal the lack of payment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which combination of duties would allow someone to create and hide a sale that never happened.

reveal.question: List the three main stages of the Order-to-Cash process covered in this lesson.

knowledgeCheck.question: What is the primary risk that the segregation of duties between credit approval and sales order entry aims to mitigate?
options: Unrecorded shipments
Extending credit to related parties
Lapping of cash receipts
Inaccurate inventory records
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The separation of credit approval and sales order entry prevents individuals from extending credit to related parties or customers who are not creditworthy. This reduces the risk of bad debt and financial losses.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who might benefit from circumventing the credit approval process.

sections.title: Billing vs. Accounts Receivable: Key Differences

rows.label: Primary Function
values: Creating and issuing invoices to customers.
Tracking customer balances and collecting payments.

rows.label: Data Input
values: Shipping information, sales orders, pricing data.
Payment information, adjustments, credit memos.

rows.label: Key Risk
values: Inaccurate invoices, unbilled shipments.
Misapplication of payments, uncollectible accounts.

title: Procurement Cycle Controls and Risks
description: Master controls over the procure-to-pay process

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The procurement cycle is the most common area for fraud and kickbacks. Understanding procure-to-pay controls is essential for CIA Part 2 and real-world internal audit practice.

sections.title: Procurement Cycle Stages
sections.content:
**Procure-to-Pay Process:**

1. **Requisition**
   • Need identification
   • Requisition preparation and approval
   • Budget verification

2. **Purchasing**
   • Vendor selection
   • Purchase order creation
   • Contract negotiation (if applicable)

3. **Receiving**
   • Goods receipt and inspection
   • Receiving report preparation
   • Discrepancy handling

4. **Accounts Payable**
   • Three-way match
   • Invoice approval
   • Payment scheduling

5. **Disbursement**
   • Check/EFT preparation
   • Payment authorization
   • Vendor remittance

sections.title: The Three-Way Match
sections.content:
**The Three-Way Match** compares:

1. **Purchase Order** - What was ordered (quantity, price, terms)
2. **Receiving Report** - What was received (quantity, condition)
3. **Vendor Invoice** - What vendor is billing

**All three documents must agree** before payment is authorized.

**Exceptions Requiring Investigation:**
• Quantity differences beyond tolerance
• Price discrepancies
• Items received but not ordered
• Invoices without purchase orders (potential fraud)

sections.title: Key Controls
rows: Prevent payment for goods not received

sections.title: Segregation of Duties
sections.content:
**Must Be Separated:**

• **Requisitioning** vs. **Purchasing** vs. **Receiving**
  (Prevents ordering for personal use)

• **Vendor file maintenance** vs. **Check preparation**
  (Prevents payments to fictitious vendors)

• **Receiving** vs. **Accounts Payable**
  (Prevents paying for goods not received)

• **Check signing** vs. **Bank reconciliation**
  (Prevents concealment of unauthorized payments)

sections.title: Vendor Fraud Red Flags
sections.content:
**Red flags for vendor fraud/kickbacks:**

• Single-source contracts without justification
• Vendor addresses match employee addresses
• Round dollar invoices
• Invoices just under approval thresholds
• Unusual volume with one vendor
• Missing competitive bids
• Frequent change orders inflating contract value

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three-way match is the primary control over disbursements
Vendor file maintenance is a high-risk function
Competitive bidding prevents kickbacks and ensures fair pricing
Check signing must be segregated from bank reconciliation
Watch for red flags indicating vendor collusion

sections.title: Test Your Knowledge: The Three-Way Match

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following discrepancies, discovered during a three-way match, would MOST likely indicate a potential internal control failure related to unauthorized purchases?
options: The vendor invoice lists a higher price per unit than the purchase order, but the receiving report confirms the correct quantity was received.
The receiving report indicates a quantity received that is less than the quantity ordered on the purchase order, but the vendor invoice matches the receiving report.
The vendor invoice includes a line item for a service not specified on the purchase order or the receiving report.
The purchase order specifies a different delivery date than the vendor invoice.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The inclusion of a service not on the purchase order or receiving report strongly suggests an unauthorized purchase. The other options could be due to pricing errors, shipping issues, or timing differences, but adding an entirely new service is a red flag.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which discrepancy directly implies something was purchased without proper authorization.

reveal.question: Why is it crucial to segregate the duties of vendor file maintenance and check preparation?
reveal.answer: To prevent payments to fictitious vendors or the alteration of vendor information for fraudulent purposes. If the same person can add or modify vendor details and then issue payments, they could create a fake vendor account and pay themselves.

rows.label: Primary Responsibility
values: Identifying and requesting the need for goods or services.
Selecting vendors, negotiating terms, and creating purchase orders.

rows.label: Key Activities
values: Determining the required quantity and specifications.
Obtaining competitive bids and evaluating vendor performance.

rows.label: Control Objective
values: Ensuring that only legitimate and necessary needs are requested.
Ensuring that goods and services are acquired at the best possible value and from reliable sources.

title: Payroll Cycle Controls and Risks
description: Master controls over payroll processing and employee compensation

sections.title: Payroll Cycle Overview
sections.content:
**Key Payroll Functions:**

1. **Human Resources**
   • Hiring and termination
   • Pay rate authorization
   • Employee master file maintenance

2. **Timekeeping**
   • Time and attendance recording
   • Supervisor approval
   • Leave management

3. **Payroll Processing**
   • Gross pay calculation
   • Deductions and withholdings
   • Net pay computation

4. **Disbursement**
   • Paycheck/EFT preparation
   • Distribution to employees
   • Tax remittances

sections.title: Segregation of Duties
sections.content:
**Critical Separations:**

• **HR (hiring)** vs. **Payroll processing**
  (Prevents ghost employees)

• **Timekeeping** vs. **Payroll calculation**
  (Prevents inflated hours)

• **Payroll preparation** vs. **Check distribution**
  (Prevents payroll check theft)

• **Payroll bank account** vs. **Regular operating account**
  (Facilitates cash control)

sections.title: Payroll Fraud Schemes
sections.content:
**Common Payroll Frauds:**

1. **Ghost Employees**
   • Fictitious employees on payroll
   • Departed employees not removed
   • Detection: Match payroll to HR, observe distribution

2. **Falsified Hours**
   • Inflated overtime
   • Hours reported not worked
   • Detection: Time analysis, supervisor review

3. **Commission Fraud**
   • Fictitious sales for commission
   • Revenue reversals after commission paid
   • Detection: Commission recalculation, reversals analysis

4. **Expense Reimbursement**
   • Fictitious expenses
   • Inflated amounts
   • Detection: Receipt verification, policy compliance

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: HR hiring function must be segregated from payroll processing
Ghost employee prevention requires matching payroll to HR records
Physical payroll distribution or direct deposit reduces theft risk
Supervisor approval of time is essential but not sufficient alone
Separate payroll bank account facilitates control and reconciliation

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following segregation of duties is MOST effective in preventing the creation of ghost employees in the payroll cycle?
options: The payroll department should not have access to blank payroll checks.
The human resources department should be separate from the payroll processing function.
The timekeeping department should be separate from the payroll calculation function.
The supervisor should not be responsible for distributing payroll checks.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The most effective control for preventing ghost employees is separating the authorization of new hires (Human Resources) from the actual payroll processing. This prevents unauthorized individuals from being added to the payroll system. While the other options are important controls, they address different risks.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which department is responsible for adding new employees to the system.

reveal.question: Describe two methods for detecting ghost employees on the payroll.
reveal.answer: 1. Matching the active payroll list to the HR employee master file to identify discrepancies. 2. Observing the payroll check distribution process to identify checks that are not claimed or appear suspicious.

rows.label: Primary Function
values: Records and approves employee work hours.
Calculates gross pay, deductions, and net pay.

rows.label: Control Objective
values: Ensures accurate recording and approval of hours worked.
Ensures accurate and timely payment to employees.

rows.label: Key Risk
values: Falsified time reports (inflated hours).
Incorrect pay calculations and unauthorized deductions.

title: ESG and Sustainability Auditing
description: Emerging topic: Auditing Environmental, Social, and Governance factors

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) is a rapidly emerging area for internal audit. Regulators worldwide are mandating climate and sustainability disclosures. The IIA has signaled ESG will be increasingly important on the CIA exam. This is a differentiating topic for world-class preparation.

sections.title: ESG Components
sections.content:
**Environmental Factors:**
• Climate risk and carbon emissions
• Energy efficiency and renewable energy
• Water usage and waste management
• Biodiversity and ecosystem impact
• Environmental compliance

**Social Factors:**
• Labor practices and human rights
• Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI)
• Employee health and safety
• Community relations and impact
• Supply chain social responsibility

**Governance Factors:**
• Board composition and independence
• Executive compensation
• Business ethics and anti-corruption
• Shareholder rights
• Risk oversight and transparency

sections.title: Key ESG Reporting Frameworks
rows: TCFD (Task Force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures)

sections.title: Internal Audit Role in ESG
sections.content:
**Internal Audit Can Provide Assurance Over:**

1. **ESG Data Quality**
   • Accuracy of emissions calculations
   • Completeness of scope 1, 2, 3 data
   • Reliability of sustainability metrics

2. **Controls Over ESG Reporting**
   • Data collection processes
   • Third-party data validation
   • Report preparation controls

3. **ESG Risk Management**
   • Climate risk assessment processes
   • Transition risk planning
   • Physical risk mitigation

4. **Governance Structures**
   • Board ESG oversight
   • ESG-linked compensation
   • Stakeholder engagement processes

sections.title: ESG Audit Challenges
sections.content:
**Unique Challenges for ESG Auditing:**

• **Immature data systems** - ESG data often not as robust as financial data
• **Multiple frameworks** - No single global mandatory standard (yet)
• **Scope 3 complexity** - Supply chain emissions difficult to measure
• **Greenwashing risk** - Misleading or exaggerated claims
• **Evolving regulations** - Requirements rapidly changing
• **Estimation uncertainty** - Many metrics require significant judgment
• **Third-party reliance** - External certifications and offsets

sections.title: TCFD Recommendations
sections.content:
**TCFD Four Core Elements:**

**1. Governance** - Board and management oversight of climate risk
**2. Strategy** - Climate-related risks and opportunities impact
**3. Risk Management** - How climate risks are identified, assessed, managed
**4. Metrics & Targets** - Metrics used to assess and manage climate risk

Internal audit should assess maturity across all four pillars.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ESG = Environmental, Social, Governance factors
Multiple reporting frameworks exist (GRI, SASB, TCFD, ISSB)
Internal audit provides assurance over ESG data quality and controls
Greenwashing is a significant risk requiring auditor attention
ESG audit requires understanding emerging regulations and frameworks

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST important consideration for an internal auditor when assessing the accuracy of a company's Scope 1 emissions data?
options: Ensuring alignment with the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) standards.
Verifying the calibration and maintenance records of emissions monitoring equipment.
Comparing the company's reported emissions to industry averages.
Reviewing the company's policies on employee travel.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Verifying the calibration and maintenance records directly addresses the reliability of the data source. While GRI alignment and industry comparisons are useful, they don't guarantee accuracy. Employee travel policies are related to Scope 3 emissions, not Scope 1.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the direct source of the emissions data.

reveal.question: List three key components of 'Environmental' factors considered in ESG auditing.
reveal.answer:
1. Climate risk and carbon emissions
2. Water usage and waste management
3. Biodiversity and ecosystem impact

rows.label: Definition
values: Direct emissions from owned or controlled sources.
Indirect emissions from the generation of purchased electricity, steam, heating, and cooling consumed by the reporting company.
All other indirect emissions that occur in a company’s value chain.

rows.label: Example
values: Emissions from a company's factory smokestacks.
Emissions from the power plant that generates electricity used in a company's office building.
Emissions from the manufacturing of goods purchased by the company, business travel, or employee commuting.

rows.label: Audit Complexity
values: Generally easier to audit due to direct control.
Moderately complex, requires understanding of energy sourcing.
Most complex due to reliance on third-party data and estimations.

knowledgeCheck.question: An internal auditor discovers that a company's sustainability report highlights a small reduction in plastic packaging while simultaneously expanding its use of non-recyclable materials in other product lines. This is MOST likely an example of:
options: Material misstatement
Inadequate segregation of duties
Greenwashing
Scope creep
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Greenwashing involves selectively highlighting positive environmental aspects while downplaying negative ones to create a misleadingly positive image. While the situation might involve material misstatement depending on the specific impact, greenwashing is the most direct and accurate description of the scenario.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the intent behind the selective reporting.

title: Third-Party and Vendor Risk Management
description: Auditing outsourced functions and vendor relationships

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Organizations increasingly rely on third parties for critical functions. Vendor risks extend to the organization - "You can outsource a function but not the risk." Third-party risk management is frequently tested on CIA Part 2.

sections.title: Third-Party Risk Categories
sections.content:
**Key Risk Categories:**

• **Operational Risk** - Service disruption, quality failures
• **Compliance Risk** - Regulatory violations by vendor
• **Financial Risk** - Vendor financial instability
• **Strategic Risk** - Over-dependence, loss of competitive advantage
• **Reputational Risk** - Vendor actions affecting brand
• **Cybersecurity Risk** - Data breaches, access vulnerabilities
• **Concentration Risk** - Reliance on single vendor
• **Country/Political Risk** - Geopolitical impacts on service

sections.title: SOC Reports Overview
sections.content:
**SOC (System and Organization Controls) Reports:**

**SOC 1** - Controls over Financial Reporting
• Type I: Design at a point in time
• Type II: Design and operating effectiveness over period
• For service organizations affecting clients' financial statements

**SOC 2** - Trust Services Criteria
• Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy
• Type I or Type II available
• Not as restricted in distribution

**SOC 3** - Public Report
• Same criteria as SOC 2
• Suitable for general distribution
• Less detailed than SOC 2

sections.title: Using SOC Reports
sections.content:
**When reviewing SOC reports:**

1. **Check scope** - Does it cover your services/locations?
2. **Review period** - Is it current? Bridge letters for gaps?
3. **Check exceptions** - Any control failures noted?
4. **Complementary controls** - What controls are YOUR responsibility?
5. **Subservice providers** - Are they carved out or included?
6. **Auditor reputation** - Is the CPA firm reputable?

sections.title: Key Contract Provisions
sections.content:
**Essential Vendor Contract Terms:**

• **Right to audit** - Access to vendor facilities and records
• **Service levels** - Performance metrics and remedies
• **Security requirements** - Specific controls required
• **Business continuity** - BCP/DR requirements and testing
• **Data protection** - Ownership, confidentiality, breach notification
• **Subcontracting** - Approval requirements, controls
• **Termination rights** - Exit triggers, data return, transition assistance
• **Insurance** - Liability coverage requirements
• **Compliance** - Regulatory obligation compliance

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: You can outsource functions but not accountability
Due diligence should occur before contract signing
SOC reports provide third-party assurance on controls
Right to audit clause enables ongoing monitoring
Exit planning should be considered before engagement

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios BEST exemplifies a strategic risk associated with third-party vendor relationships?
options: A vendor experiences a data breach, compromising sensitive customer information.
A critical vendor's financial instability leads to service disruptions.
An organization becomes overly reliant on a single vendor for a key service, hindering its ability to innovate and adapt to market changes.
A vendor's non-compliance with environmental regulations results in fines for the organization.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Strategic risk focuses on the long-term impact on the organization's competitive position and ability to achieve its objectives. Over-dependence on a single vendor limits flexibility and innovation, fitting this description.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which risk category deals with the long-term competitive advantage of the organization.

reveal.question: What is the primary focus of a SOC 1 report?
reveal.answer: Controls at a service organization relevant to user entities' internal control over financial reporting.

sections.title: SOC 1 Type I vs. SOC 1 Type II

rows.label: Scope
values: Design of controls at a specific point in time
Design and operating effectiveness of controls over a period of time

rows.label: Assurance Level
values: Provides assurance on the suitability of the design of controls
Provides assurance on both the suitability of design and the operating effectiveness of controls

rows.label: Time Period
values: Point in time
Specified period (e.g., six months, one year)

rows.label: Use Case
values: Initial assessment of a service organization's controls
Ongoing monitoring and assessment of a service organization's controls
